claims.claim_text: The opening scene places Tianqi Chen in Seattle in August 2014 at 2:17 AM.
claims.span_hint: section: The Algorithm That Quietly Runs the World
claims.anchor_text: **Seattle, Washington. August 2014. 2:17 AM.**
claims.suggested_rewrite: **Early XGBoost era.**
claims.rationale: This is a cinematic historical setup with precise time-and-place detail and no citable source in the module.

claims.claim_text: XGBoost had just won another Kaggle competition on a major-bank credit-card-fraud dataset for a $10,000 prize.
claims.span_hint: section: The Algorithm That Quietly Runs the World
claims.anchor_text: Tianqi Chen stared at his screen, watching the numbers scroll by. His new algorithm—XGBoost—had just won another Kaggle competition. Not by a little, but by a lot. The dataset? Credit card fraud detection for a major bank. The prize? $10,000. But more importantly, the implications.
claims.suggested_rewrite: By the mid-2010s, XGBoost was gaining attention for strong results on structured-data problems and ML competitions.
claims.rationale: This is a specific anecdote with named person, event framing, and quantitative detail that is not backed by a cited primary source.

claims.claim_text: XGBoost soon won virtually every tabular Kaggle competition and companies like Airbnb, Uber, and Amazon replaced neural networks with gradient boosting for pricing and fraud detection.
claims.span_hint: section: The Algorithm That Quietly Runs the World
claims.anchor_text: Within two years, XGBoost would win virtually every tabular data competition on Kaggle. Companies like Airbnb, Uber, and Amazon quietly replaced their neural networks with gradient boosting for everything from pricing to fraud detection. The algorithm that academia dismissed as "just trees" became the engine of modern business AI.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Over the next few years, XGBoost became one of the most influential tools in tabular machine learning, and gradient boosting became a standard baseline for many business prediction tasks.
claims.rationale: These are sweeping, specific adoption claims about named companies and competition dominance without an authoritative source.

claims.claim_text: About 80% of production ML systems use tree-based models on tabular data.
claims.span_hint: section: Why Tabular ML Still Matters
claims.anchor_text: **~80% of production ML systems use tree-based models on tabular data.**
claims.suggested_rewrite: **A large share of production ML for structured business data relies on tree-based models.**
claims.rationale: This exact percentage is a load-bearing statistic and needs a source; no allowlisted primary source was found for this specific number.

claims.claim_text: Gradient boosting wins about 70% of Kaggle tabular competitions.
claims.span_hint: section: Why Tabular ML Still Matters
claims.anchor_text: **Did You Know?** In Kaggle competitions on tabular data, gradient boosting methods (XGBoost, LightGBM, CatBoost) win approximately 70% of the time. Deep learning rarely beats them on structured data, despite years of research into "deep learning for tabular data."
claims.suggested_rewrite: **Did You Know?** In tabular-data competitions and benchmarks, gradient boosting is often the strongest baseline, and deep learning frequently struggles to beat it on structured data.
claims.rationale: The exact win-rate figure is a statistic that requires support and should not remain as an uncited percentage.

claims.claim_text: Ross Quinlan created ID3 in 1986, later developed C4.5, and C4.5 became one of the top 10 data-mining algorithms ever.
claims.anchor_text: **Did You Know?** The first decision tree algorithm (ID3) was created by Ross Quinlan in 1986. He later developed C4.5, which became one of the top 10 data mining algorithms ever. His work was done at the University of Sydney and his algorithms remain foundational to modern ML.
claims.suggested_rewrite: **Did You Know?** ID3 and later decision-tree work such as C4.5 helped establish decision trees as one of the foundational toolkits in classical machine learning.
claims.rationale: This sentence combines real historical facts with an uncited ranking claim, making the composite version unsafe to keep as written.

claims.claim_text: Leo Breiman invented Random Forest at UC Berkeley in 2001, also invented bagging and CART, and famously argued prediction mattered more than interpretability.
claims.anchor_text: **Did You Know?** Random Forest was invented by Leo Breiman at UC Berkeley in 2001. Breiman was a legendary statistician who also invented bagging and CART (Classification and Regression Trees). He famously criticized the statistics community for being too focused on simple models, arguing that prediction accuracy should matter more than interpretability.
claims.suggested_rewrite: **Did You Know?** Random Forest emerged in the early 2000s from Leo Breiman's work on tree ensembles and became a standard baseline for tabular ML.
claims.rationale: The paragraph bundles several historical and evaluative claims that would need multiple precise sources.

claims.claim_text: Jerome Friedman developed gradient boosting at Stanford in 2001, his paper is one of the most cited ML papers ever, and he co-authored The Elements of Statistical Learning.
claims.span_hint: section: Gradient Boosting: The Competition Winner
claims.anchor_text: **Did You Know?** The gradient boosting algorithm was developed by Jerome Friedman at Stanford in 2001. His paper "Greedy Function Approximation: A Gradient Boosting Machine" is one of the most cited ML papers ever. Friedman also created MARS (Multivariate Adaptive Regression Splines) and co-authored "The Elements of Statistical Learning," the bible of classical ML.
claims.suggested_rewrite: **Did You Know?** Gradient boosting was formalized by Jerome Friedman in the early 2000s and became one of the foundational ideas behind modern tree ensembles.
claims.rationale: This is another composite history paragraph with an uncited citation-rank superlative.

claims.claim_text: LightGBM was introduced by Microsoft in 2017 and is faster than XGBoost on large datasets because of GOSS, EFB, histogram binning, and leaf-wise growth.
claims.span_hint: section: The Big Three: XGBoost, LightGBM, CatBoost
claims.rationale: This is real and verifiable in the LightGBM paper, but the primary source sits on a host not in the current allowlist.

claims.claim_text: CatBoost handles categorical features natively, uses ordered boosting to reduce target leakage, defaults to symmetric trees, and supports GPU training.
claims.span_hint: section: The Big Three: XGBoost, LightGBM, CatBoost
claims.rationale: CatBoost's official docs and paper references directly support these library-specific claims.

claims.claim_text: XGBoost was created by Tianqi Chen at the University of Washington, became synonymous with winning Kaggle competitions, and Chen later co-created Apache TVM and MXNet.
claims.span_hint: section: The Big Three: XGBoost, LightGBM, CatBoost
claims.anchor_text: **Did You Know?** XGBoost was created by Tianqi Chen as a research project at the University of Washington. It became so dominant that for several years, "XGBoost" was practically synonymous with "winning Kaggle competition." Chen later co-created Apache TVM and MXNet.
claims.suggested_rewrite: **Did You Know?** XGBoost began as a research project led by Tianqi Chen and became one of the best-known gradient-boosting libraries in applied machine learning.
claims.rationale: The first clause is supportable, but the composite paragraph adds uncited dominance language and extra biography claims.

claims.claim_text: Optuna was created by Preferred Networks, uses TPE, and its name comes from optimize plus tuna.
claims.anchor_text: **Did You Know?** Optuna was created by Preferred Networks, a Japanese AI startup. It uses sophisticated algorithms like Tree-structured Parzen Estimator (TPE) to explore hyperparameter space efficiently. The name comes from "optimize" + "tuna" (a fish that swims efficiently through water, like the algorithm through parameter space).
claims.suggested_rewrite: **Did You Know?** Optuna is a popular hyperparameter-optimization library designed to search large parameter spaces efficiently.
claims.rationale: The paragraph mixes product history, algorithm detail, and etymology without a clean allowlisted primary source.

claims.claim_text: SHAP adapts Shapley values from cooperative game theory into feature-attribution methods for ML predictions.
claims.rationale: The SHAP paper and the Shapley-value reference directly support this linkage.

claims.claim_text: Lloyd Shapley won the Nobel Memorial Prize in Economic Sciences in 2012.
claims.rationale: The referenced page directly states the award year and laureate.

claims.claim_text: The insurance-leakage example is set in Chicago in February 2022.
claims.span_hint: section: Production War Stories: Gradient Boosting Gone Wrong
claims.anchor_text: **Chicago. February 2022. Insurance company underwriting.**
claims.suggested_rewrite: **Example: data leakage in insurance underwriting.**
claims.rationale: This is a real-event framing with precise date and location but no source.

claims.claim_text: An insurer lost $18 million in unexpected claims after deploying an XGBoost pricing model with leakage.
claims.span_hint: section: Production War Stories: Gradient Boosting Gone Wrong
claims.anchor_text: By Q3, the company had lost $18 million in unexpected claims. What went wrong?
claims.suggested_rewrite: After deployment, the team discovered that severe data leakage had made the model look much better offline than it really was in production.
claims.rationale: This is a quantified incident claim and must be sourced or rewritten to remove the false precision.

claims.claim_text: The fraud-accuracy example is set in Singapore in 2023.
claims.span_hint: section: Production War Stories: Gradient Boosting Gone Wrong
claims.anchor_text: **Singapore. 2023. E-commerce payments.**
claims.suggested_rewrite: **Example: misleading accuracy on an imbalanced fraud dataset.**
claims.rationale: This is again a specific real-event framing without a source.

claims.claim_text: A LightGBM fraud model achieved 99.9% accuracy only because 99.9% of transactions were legitimate and the model predicted no fraud.
claims.span_hint: section: Production War Stories: Gradient Boosting Gone Wrong
claims.anchor_text: A team deployed a LightGBM fraud detection model with 99.9% accuracy. Executives celebrated. Then they looked at the confusion matrix.
claims.suggested_rewrite: A team deployed a fraud model with superficially excellent accuracy, only to discover that class imbalance made the metric almost meaningless.
claims.rationale: The precise percentage and scenario are presented as a real incident but have no backing.

claims.claim_text: The credit-fairness example is set in Austin in 2021.
claims.span_hint: section: Production War Stories: Gradient Boosting Gone Wrong
claims.anchor_text: **Austin. 2021. Credit scoring startup.**
claims.suggested_rewrite: **Example: proxy discrimination in credit scoring.**
claims.rationale: This named time-and-place framing would require a real source.

claims.claim_text: Using zip code improved AUC because it proxied race, violating ECOA and triggering a regulatory investigation.
claims.span_hint: section: Production War Stories: Gradient Boosting Gone Wrong
claims.anchor_text: Zip codes in the US are highly correlated with race. The model had effectively learned to discriminate by race—a violation of the Equal Credit Opportunity Act. The company faced regulatory investigation and had to rebuild their entire model.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Location-like features can proxy for protected characteristics, so credit models should be audited for fairness and regulatory risk before deployment.
claims.rationale: The specific allegation and investigation are unsourced, so the rewrite should keep the fairness lesson and drop the incident details.

claims.claim_text: The Equal Credit Opportunity Act prohibits certain forms of discrimination in credit transactions, including on the basis of race.
claims.span_hint: section: Production War Stories: Gradient Boosting Gone Wrong
claims.rationale: The ABA consumer-law explainer directly summarizes ECOA's protected bases.

claims.claim_text: Training tree models uses any laptop CPU while neural-network training costs $1-10 per GPU hour.
claims.anchor_text: | **Training hardware** | CPU (any laptop) | GPU ($1-10/hr) |
claims.suggested_rewrite: | **Training hardware** | Often CPU-friendly | Often benefits from accelerator hardware |
claims.rationale: This table row contains specific pricing and hardware generalizations without a source.

claims.claim_text: For 1M rows, gradient boosting trains in 5-30 minutes while neural networks take 2-8 hours.
claims.anchor_text: | **Training time (1M rows)** | 5-30 minutes | 2-8 hours |
claims.suggested_rewrite: | **Training time (1M rows)** | Often much shorter | Often longer and more tuning-sensitive |
claims.rationale: These exact benchmark ranges are not supported by a cited benchmark methodology.

claims.claim_text: A 2023 survey found 67% of production business models were tree-based.
claims.anchor_text: **Did You Know?** According to a 2023 survey of ML practitioners, 67% of production models for business applications are tree-based. The remaining 33% are split between neural networks, linear models, and other approaches. Trees dominate because they're fast, interpretable, and good enough.
claims.suggested_rewrite: **Did You Know?** For many business applications, tree-based models remain a very common production choice because they are fast, interpretable, and strong on structured data.
claims.rationale: The exact survey breakdown is a statistic that needs a source, not an uncited percentage split.

claims.claim_text: Kaggle winners typically get 80% of gains from feature engineering and 20% from model selection or tuning.
claims.span_hint: section: Feature Engineering for Gradient Boosting
claims.anchor_text: **Did You Know?** In competitive ML (Kaggle), winners report that 80% of their performance gain typically comes from feature engineering, while only 20% comes from model selection and hyperparameter tuning. Yet most practitioners spend 80% of their time on modeling and 20% on features. Flip that ratio for better results.
claims.suggested_rewrite: **Did You Know?** In competitive tabular ML, feature engineering often matters at least as much as marginal model or tuning changes.
claims.rationale: The 80/20 split is a precise statistic and should not stand without evidence.

claims.claim_text: About 80% of production ML uses tree-based models on structured data, including fraud detection, credit scoring, ranking, and churn prediction at most major companies.
claims.anchor_text: 1. **Trees dominate tabular ML** - Despite deep learning hype, ~80% of production ML uses tree-based models on structured data. This includes fraud detection, credit scoring, recommendation ranking, and churn prediction at most major companies.
claims.suggested_rewrite: 1. **Trees dominate tabular ML** - Despite deep learning hype, tree-based models remain a common production choice for structured-data problems such as fraud detection, credit scoring, ranking, and churn prediction.
claims.rationale: This repeats the earlier unsupported percentage and broadens it with more uncited scope.

claims.claim_text: A LightGBM model can serve sub-millisecond latency on a single CPU core.
claims.anchor_text: 9. **Production deployment is straightforward** - Tree models are small, fast, and don't need GPUs for inference. A LightGBM model can serve sub-millisecond latency on a single CPU core.
claims.suggested_rewrite: 9. **Production deployment is straightforward** - Tree models are usually compact, fast, and often deploy well on CPU-only inference stacks.
claims.rationale: The exact latency benchmark is uncited and hardware-dependent.

claims.claim_text: The Airbnb example is framed as a real company case study.
claims.anchor_text: ### Airbnb's Search Ranking
claims.suggested_rewrite: ### Example: Marketplace Search Ranking
claims.rationale: Without a source, the heading should not present this as a verified company case.

claims.claim_text: Airbnb uses LightGBM at the core of search ranking over hundreds of features and serves 300+ million requests per day with P99 latency under 50ms.
claims.anchor_text: Airbnb uses gradient boosting at the core of their search ranking system. When you search for a place to stay, LightGBM models rank results based on hundreds of features: listing attributes, user preferences, historical booking patterns, and pricing. The model serves 300+ million requests per day with P99 latency under 50ms.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Large marketplace search systems are a good example of ranking problems where gradient boosting can be effective, but exact deployment metrics should be sourced case by case.
claims.rationale: This is a named-company operational claim with exact scale and latency numbers but no primary source.

claims.claim_text: The Uber example is framed as a real company case study.
claims.anchor_text: ### Uber's Surge Pricing
claims.suggested_rewrite: ### Example: Dynamic Pricing
claims.rationale: The heading should not assert a specific company deployment without support.

claims.claim_text: Uber uses XGBoost for surge pricing by predicting supply and demand every few minutes and retrains models daily.
claims.anchor_text: Uber's dynamic pricing system uses XGBoost to predict supply and demand across city regions. Every few minutes, models predict: How many ride requests will we get in this area? How many drivers will be available? The difference determines surge multipliers.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Dynamic-pricing systems often combine short-horizon demand and supply forecasts, and tree-based models can be a practical fit for that kind of structured prediction problem.
claims.rationale: This is a specific named-company system description without a cited engineering source.

claims.claim_text: The Capital One example is framed as a real company case study.
claims.anchor_text: ### Capital One's Credit Decisions
claims.suggested_rewrite: ### Example: Credit Underwriting
claims.rationale: The heading asserts a named-company case with no source.

claims.claim_text: Capital One uses gradient boosting for real-time credit decisions over thousands of features, with SHAP supporting explanations to loan officers.
claims.anchor_text: Capital One was an early adopter of ML for credit underwriting. Their gradient boosting models evaluate credit applications in real-time, considering thousands of features while remaining explainable for regulatory compliance. SHAP values help loan officers explain why applications were approved or denied.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Credit-underwriting systems often favor structured-data models that balance predictive power with explainability requirements, and feature-attribution tools can help support decision review.
claims.rationale: This is a concrete named-company deployment description that requires a source.

claims.claim_text: The Netflix example is framed as a real company case study.
claims.anchor_text: ### Netflix's Recommendation Engine
claims.suggested_rewrite: ### Example: Recommendation Ranking
claims.rationale: The heading presents a specific company deployment without citation support.

claims.claim_text: Netflix uses XGBoost as its core ranking model after candidate generation, scoring 500 titles in under 100ms.
claims.anchor_text: While Netflix's famous recommendation system uses deep learning for visual features and embeddings, their core ranking model is gradient boosting. After candidate generation produces 500 potential titles, XGBoost ranks them based on user behavior patterns, viewing history, and content features. The model must score in under 100ms to maintain responsive UI.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Recommendation systems often use multi-stage pipelines in which a fast structured-data ranking model scores a set of generated candidates before display.
claims.rationale: This is a precise system-design and latency claim about a named company with no cited evidence.

claims.claim_text: The Spotify example is framed as a real company case study.
claims.anchor_text: ### Spotify's Discover Weekly
claims.suggested_rewrite: ### Example: Music Recommendation Ranking
claims.rationale: The heading names a specific company product without a source.

claims.claim_text: Spotify's Discover Weekly uses LightGBM in the final ranking stage and processes 400 million users weekly.
claims.anchor_text: Spotify's beloved Discover Weekly playlist uses a hybrid approach, but gradient boosting is central to the final ranking step. After collaborative filtering generates candidate songs, LightGBM ranks them based on listening patterns, skip rates, and audio features. The system processes 400 million users weekly.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Music-recommendation systems often use hybrid pipelines in which a structured-data ranking model helps reorder candidates produced by collaborative or embedding-based methods.
claims.rationale: This is a named-company pipeline description with a large quantitative usage claim and no source.

claims.claim_text: A Kaggle State of ML survey says gradient boosting wins 70% of tabular competitions, with the remainder split evenly across neural nets, linear models, and hybrid ensembles.
claims.anchor_text: **Did You Know?** According to Kaggle's State of ML survey, gradient boosting models win 70% of tabular data competitions. The remaining 30% are split between neural networks (10%), linear models (10%), and ensembles of boosting + neural networks (10%). When time is limited, gradient boosting is almost always the best first choice.
claims.suggested_rewrite: **Did You Know?** In tabular-data competitions, gradient boosting is usually one of the strongest first baselines to try when time is limited.
claims.rationale: This is an uncited survey breakdown with multiple exact percentages.

claims.claim_text: Bergstra and Bengio (2012) showed random search beats grid search in 95% of cases while using only 60% of the compute.
claims.anchor_text: **Did You Know?** According to research by Bergstra and Bengio (2012), random search finds better hyperparameters than grid search in 95% of cases, while using only 60% of the compute time. The key insight: most hyperparameters don't matter much, and random search explores the important ones more efficiently.
claims.suggested_rewrite: **Did You Know?** Classical hyperparameter-search research showed that random search can be much more efficient than grid search because only a few parameters usually dominate performance.
claims.rationale: The exact percentages are unsupported here and should be removed unless backed by a precise citation.

further_reading.title: XGBoost: A Scalable Tree Boosting System
further_reading.why_relevant: Primary paper for XGBoost's core algorithmic and systems contributions.

further_reading.title: LightGBM Advanced Topics
further_reading.why_relevant: Official docs covering LightGBM's handling of missing values and categorical features.

further_reading.title: A Unified Approach to Interpreting Model Predictions
further_reading.why_relevant: Primary SHAP paper connecting Shapley-value ideas to model explanations.

notes: Highest-risk areas are the opening anecdote, the three 'production war stories,' and the five named-company 'success stories': they are written as real historical cases with concrete details but are not sourceable from the module itself. The unsupported 80%/70%/67%/95% statistics also recur in multiple sections and should be rewritten or cited consistently.
